title: Air-fryer chicken thighs
subtitle: Bone-in, skin-on, crisp in twenty-five minutes.
excerpt: Bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs rubbed with oil, salt, and a smoked paprika spice mix, then cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for twenty-five minutes. Crisp dark skin, tender meat to the bone, almost no washing-up. Feeds four with a side salad and some bread.
sourceNotes:
Air-fryer cookery is too recent for public-domain primary sources; the manufacturer guidance from Ninja, Tower, Cosori, and Tefal covers temperature and time recommendations, though it isn't citable as public domain. The recipe here is calibrated for common UK domestic air-fryers (3.5–6 litre capacity).

The internal cooking temperature for poultry (74°C / 165°F in the thickest part) draws on US Department of Agriculture and UK Food Standards Agency public guidance.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked thighs lose their crisp skin when reheated from frozen. Cook fresh.
recipe.batchNotes: The air fryer wants a single layer of thighs in the basket. Eight thighs in a 6-litre fryer is the upper limit; smaller fryers do four at a time and need to be cooked in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The spice mix can be made up and stored in a jar for a month. Rubbing the thighs the night before and resting them uncovered in the fridge dries the skin and gives a crisper finish in the air fryer.
recipe.temperatureNote: the air fryer; matches the 200°C / 392°F setting on Ninja, Tower, Cosori, and Tefal models

recipeTools.notes: Calibrated for a 3.5–6-litre domestic model. Larger or smaller capacities may need a few minutes more or less; check internal temperature with a probe.
For tossing the thighs in oil and spice.
Takes all doubt out of doneness. 74°C in the thickest part of the thigh is the target.

glossaryTerms.definition: A compact countertop convection oven with a powerful fan and a perforated basket. Hot air circulates around food at high speed, crisping the outside without the deep oil bath of traditional frying. The capacity is the limiting factor; food has to sit in a single layer for the air to reach every face.

content.text: Bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs go from packet to plate in twenty-five minutes in the
air fryer

. A two-minute rub with oil, salt, and a smoked-paprika spice mix; twenty-five minutes at 200°C; five minutes rest. Crisp dark skin, meat tender to the bone, almost no washing-up. Dinner for four with a salad and some bread.

Bone-in matters. The bone insulates the deep meat so it stays juicy through the high heat, and it carries flavour into the muscle as the meat cooks. Skinless or boneless thighs are quicker but drier; for an air-fryer dinner this casual, the small extra cook time is worth the difference.

The thighs go into the basket skin-side up. The air fryer's fan blasts hot air across the surface, which dries and crisps the skin in a way no oven can match in the same time. The basket has to hold the thighs in a single layer with a centimetre of space between each, or the air can't get to every face. A 6-litre fryer takes six to eight thighs; smaller fryers do four at a time and need a second batch.

attrs.title: Why no preheat is fine for an air fryer
attrs.body: Conventional ovens take fifteen minutes to come up to temperature; the air fryer takes about three. Some manufacturers recommend preheating, some don't. In practice it makes a small difference to the first minute or two of cooking and almost none to the finished thigh. Put the thighs in cold, set the timer, walk away. If your fryer specifies a preheat, follow it; the result is the same.

content.text: What you need

The only specialist tool is the air fryer itself. A medium mixing bowl for the oil-and-spice rub and a pair of tongs for turning the thighs cover the rest. A meat thermometer is optional but takes the doubt out of doneness; 74°C in the thickest part of the thigh is the target.
Method
Mix the rub and coat the thighs

Pat the chicken thighs dry with kitchen paper; wet skin won't crisp. In a medium bowl, mix {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{garlic}} grated garlic cloves, {{black-pepper}} of freshly ground black pepper, and {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano. Tip the chicken thighs into the bowl and turn them over with your hands until every piece is coated, working the rub under the skin where you can.
Arrange in the basket

Place the thighs in the air-fryer basket skin-side up, in a single layer with a centimetre of space between each. Don't stack them. If they don't all fit in one layer, cook in two batches; the first batch can rest, covered loosely with foil, while the second cooks.
Cook at 200°C for twenty-five minutes

Set the air fryer to 200°C and the timer to twenty-five minutes. Don't open the basket for the first fifteen minutes; the air has to keep circulating to crisp the skin. At fifteen minutes, open and check: the skin should be deeply gold and shrinking back from the meat at the edges. Close and finish the cook for the last ten minutes.
Check doneness

Test the thickest thigh with a meat thermometer; 74°C in the centre is done. Without a thermometer, push the tip of a knife in next to the bone of the thickest thigh and watch the juices run; they should run clear, not pink. If pink, return for two minutes and check again.
Rest, then serve

Lift the thighs out with tongs onto a warm plate and rest for five minutes. The skin stays crisp; the meat relaxes so the juices stay in the muscle rather than running onto the plate. Serve with a green salad, some crusty bread, and a wedge of lemon to squeeze over.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Air-fryer chicken thighs trip mostly on skin crispness and even cooking. Most problems trace to overloading the basket or wet skin.

items.symptom: Skin pale and flabby rather than crisp.
items.cause: Wet skin going in, or basket overloaded.
items.fix: Pat the thighs dry before the rub. Resting them uncovered in the fridge for an hour or overnight dries the skin further and gives the best crisp. The basket needs space between each piece; cook in two batches if you have to.

items.symptom: Some pieces cooked through, others still pink.
items.cause: Thighs different sizes, or basket overloaded.
items.fix: Pick thighs of similar size if you can. If they vary, put the largest at the edges of the basket where the airflow is strongest, and check those last with the thermometer. A second short blast covers any pieces that lag.

items.symptom: Skin burnt before the meat cooked.
items.cause: Air fryer runs hot; some models overshoot the set temperature.
items.fix: Drop the temperature to 190°C and add five minutes to the time. The lower temperature crisps without scorching. If your fryer regularly overshoots, run an oven thermometer inside one cycle and calibrate.

items.symptom: Smoke pouring out of the fryer halfway through.
items.cause: Rendered fat hitting the heating element.
items.fix: Lift the basket out, pour a tablespoon of water into the drip tray, and replace. The water cools the fat and stops it smoking. This is normal for fatty meats; the next batch goes in over the watered drip tray from the start.

content.text: Variations

Spiced versions are easy: a teaspoon of ground cumin, coriander, and turmeric in place of the paprikas gives a tikka-leaning flavour; a teaspoon of jerk seasoning plus a pinch of allspice gives a Caribbean angle. Drumsticks work the same way; allow an extra five minutes since the bone is thicker. Bone-in chicken breasts take about twenty minutes; check at fifteen since the white meat overcooks faster.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The spice mix keeps in a jar for a month and saves a minute on every cook after the first. Rubbed thighs hold in the fridge for up to twenty-four hours uncovered, which dries the skin and crisps it better in the fryer. Cooked thighs don't freeze well; the skin turns flabby on reheating. Cold leftovers shred well into a salad or onto a sandwich the next day.
Where this dish lives

The air fryer arrived in British kitchens around 2010 as a Philips product called the AirFryer; the Ninja Foodi line and the Tower and Cosori machines followed. By the early 2020s the category was the dominant new-kitchen-gadget category in the UK. The appeal was straightforward: a smaller, faster, less wasteful version of an oven, well-suited to families cooking for two or four rather than for eight.

Chicken thighs are the one dish that justifies the appliance most cleanly. A conventional oven roast takes forty-five minutes from cold; the air fryer does the same job in twenty-five with crisper skin and no warm-up. The pattern repeats across most fatty bone-in meats: pork belly, sausages, chicken wings. The high airflow does work an oven needs a much longer time to match.
